summary:
The Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 was a thirteen-day confrontation between the United States and the Soviet Union over the presence of Soviet nuclear missiles in Cuba, widely considered the closest the world came to nuclear war during the [[event:113|Cold War]]. From October 16 to 28, President John F. Kennedy and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev engaged in a tense standoff, mediated through diplomatic back-channels and direct correspondence, that ended with the removal of Soviet missiles from Cuba in exchange for American pledges not to invade the island and the secret removal of U.S. Jupiter missiles from Turkey.

The crisis originated in the Soviet decision, taken in summer 1962, to deploy medium-range and intermediate-range ballistic missiles to Cuba, capable of striking most major American cities with nuclear warheads. Khrushchev's motivations included redressing the strategic nuclear imbalance, protecting Cuba from another American invasion following the failed [[event:3348|Bay of Pigs]] operation, and demonstrating Soviet resolve to allies and adversaries. American U-2 reconnaissance aircraft photographed the missile sites on October 14, triggering a secret deliberation process within the Kennedy administration.

Kennedy was advised by a group called ExComm (Executive Committee of the National Security Council), which debated options ranging from a surgical air strike to a naval blockade to diplomatic negotiation. Kennedy ultimately chose a naval quarantine — strategically renamed a "blockade" — of Cuba to prevent additional Soviet ships from delivering more weapons. On October 22, Kennedy announced the discovery of the missiles in a nationally televised address, demanding their removal and warning of a military response if Soviet ships attempted to breach the quarantine.

For six days, Soviet ships carrying military cargo sailed toward the quarantine line. The moment they turned back — which Secretary of State Dean Rusk described as eyeball-to-eyeball confrontation ending when the other side blinked — became one of the Cold War's defining images. Behind the scenes, Kennedy and Khrushchev exchanged letters, with Khrushchev initially offering to remove missiles in exchange for U.S. non-invasion pledges and then adding the demand for removal of Jupiter missiles from Turkey. Kennedy secretly accepted both conditions while publicly accepting only the non-invasion pledge.

The crisis revealed how close to nuclear war the superpowers could stumble. One Soviet submarine commander, Vasili Arkhipov, prevented the launch of a nuclear torpedo by refusing to authorize its firing when his submarine was depth-charged by American naval vessels — a decision that may have saved the world from nuclear exchange. The crisis prompted the creation of the Moscow-Washington hotline in June 1963 and led to the [[event:3360|Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty]] later that year.

The Cuban Missile Crisis is studied as a case study in crisis management, decision-making under extreme pressure, and the dangers of nuclear deterrence. It demonstrated that even careful, intelligent leaders can come dangerously close to catastrophic miscalculation during international crises, and that chance and individual decisions can determine outcomes of world-historical consequence. The crisis strengthened Kennedy's reputation and chastened both superpowers into pursuing arms control negotiations.
